title: Seeker's Guide to Volcanoes, Space, and Sunlight
summary: Uncle Grisco visits Seeker to explore the wonders of the universe. Their scientific journey begins deep within the Earth, explaining how molten lava erupts through volcanoes to form basalt, obsidian, and pumice. The conversation then turns to the cosmos, discussing the atmosphere that ignites shooting stars and the low-gravity landscapes of the moon. Finally, Seeker explains the miracle of photosynthesis, revealing how leaves harness sunlight and air to help trees grow.

highlightQuote.text: But in order to find out, you have to keep telling stories and then you'll find out.

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: I want a Seeker story.

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: Ding dong, Seeker. Seeker, the doorbell rang.
Oh, who is it? says Seeker.
transcript.speaker: Dad
Izzy
transcript.text: It's Uncle Grisco.

transcript.speaker: Dad
transcript.text: Oh, come on in, Uncle Grisco.

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: I want to learn about volcanoes, says Uncle Grisco.

transcript.speaker: Dad
transcript.text: You do? Well, you know, I have books about volcanoes and pictures of volcanoes and things, said Seeker.
Can you show me one of them?
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: And I have a model of a volcano here as well.

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: Then can you try to give it to me? But I will hold it very gently and then give it back to you.

Oh, okay. And so Seeker pulled out a beautifully carved stone model volcano. It was about the size of your expedition ship, but instead of the shape of a ship, it had a cone shape with a flattened top that had a hole in it. So it's like a mountain but with a hole right down the middle.
transcript.speaker: Dad
Dad
transcript.text: And inside that hole was some red glowing stuff. And do you know what that was, Izzy?
Lava.
transcript.speaker: Izzy

transcript.text: That's the lava. And when a volcano erupts, it's the lava coming up from underground.
transcript.speaker: Dad
Izzy
transcript.text: Well, the volcano has to be sitting under the lava in order and then to find a way to bring it out the top.

transcript.speaker: Dad
transcript.text: Well, Seeker said, yes, that's an interesting point, Uncle Grisco. The way it works is that there's just lava deep underground all the time. It's very, very deep, so it's far, far away from us. There's lots of dirt and then lots and lots of stone underneath us, but below that, you know, the whole planet that we're on, Earth, is made of rock, of stone. But as you go down deeper and deeper, it gets hotter and hotter. And so the rock down there is molten rock, it's lava. And sometimes there are cracks or channels or tubes in that rock and the lava can come up. And when the lava comes up, it's like getting squeezed up out of... like your popsicle. It's getting squeezed from the bottom by all the heat down there of the lava underneath the rock and it just squirts out. Or it's more like one of your pouches, a yogurt pouch, being squeezed from the bottom and it just squirts out the top. And so the lava comes from deep underground and it just shoots out from the top of the volcano.
Just like when... just like when there's shooting stars.
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: Hmm, that's interesting, said Seeker. Now, a shooting star is a different matter altogether. A shooting star is a glowing streak in the sky that you see because a tiny rock from far away in outer space has fallen close enough to our planet to go through the atmosphere. The atmosphere is the gas, the air that we breathe. And when the rock comes into the atmosphere, it heats up, and then it starts to burn up, and as it burns, it makes light. That's what makes that glowing streak. That's a shooting star.
Dad, when you put your face under the blanket, the stars look different.
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: Oh, let me try. Here, we can put both our faces under. Oh, yeah, it does look a little different. Hmm. Now, back to volcanoes. One other interesting thing about the lava: it starts out as rock and then the heat inside the Earth melts it because it gets so hot. And then it shoots up out of the volcano, and then it pours out the top of the volcano, and you know what happens to it next?

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: No.

It cools down, and then it hardens back into rock. That's what we call volcanic rock. Do you know some kinds of volcanic rock? One is called basalt, it's like hard and gray. One is called obsidian, it's black and shiny. We have a piece of obsidian. And another one is called pumice, it's gray and rough with little air bubbles in it.
transcript.speaker: Dad
Izzy
transcript.text: I have one of those in the bathroom. Is that volcanic?

transcript.speaker: Dad
transcript.text: It's a volcanic rock, yeah.

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: Duh.
Well, on Mars there are volcanoes.
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: Yeah, that's true.

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: Nobody lives on Mars.

transcript.speaker: Dad
transcript.text: Yeah, nobody lives there, that's true too.
What about Venus?
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: Nope, nobody lives there either. People only live on the Earth, said Seeker.
Well, astronauts go to the moon.
transcript.speaker: Izzy

transcript.text: Sometimes they do. Not very often.
transcript.speaker: Dad

transcript.text: And there is no gravity. So people bounce when they walk around on the moon.
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: Well, yeah, there's less gravity there, so you bounce when you go on the moon, that's true.
Yeah, just like Gravity Goldfish.
transcript.speaker: Izzy

transcript.text: Just like Gravity Goldfish?
transcript.speaker: Dad
Izzy
transcript.text: When things are flying up in the air.

transcript.speaker: Dad
transcript.text: Hmm. And Seeker said, well, Uncle Grisco, I've enjoyed talking about volcanoes and shooting stars and outer space with you. Do you have any other questions?
Well, I also want to learn about leaves.
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: Oh, about leaves? What would you like to learn about them?

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: I don't know, but in order to do that, you have to... but in order to find out, you have to keep telling stories and then you'll find out.
Oh, okay. Well, Seeker said, leaves are very interesting. Now, why do you think trees make leaves? Do you know?
transcript.speaker: Dad

transcript.text: No, I don't.
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: Well, leaves actually do a lot of things, but the main thing that they do is they catch the light of the sun, which the tree uses to get energy to grow. The trees use the light of the sun and carbon dioxide from the air and water from the rain to make more tree. That's how they grow. They grow their wood and their bark and their leaves and their roots just by combining those things: the sun and the air and the water. And that's how all plants grow. And so the leaves are very important for the tree because that's how they get the sunlight. There's a process called photosynthesis, which is how they take the sunlight and turn it into what they need to use to grow.
Dad? After the winter, in the summertime or springtime, I will plant new seeds because I have a whole new pot of dirt right outside.
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: Oh, that sounds great. What plants are going to grow from those seeds?

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: Flowers and carrots.
That sounds wonderful.
transcript.speaker: Dad
Izzy
transcript.text: Actually not carrots. Because they live underground.
Yeah, but they grow from seeds too. All right. I think that's the end of the stars and stories.
transcript.speaker: Dad

characters.name: Seeker
characters.description: A brave dwarven explorer and mediator, he resides in a glowing castle hub—a multiversal dock for skyships anchored within the skyline of New York City. From this urban sanctum, he conducts silver slime experiments and tends to wandering cats amidst a massive library. Frequently swapping between a red coat and a mystical purple cloak, his braided red beard remains his most eccentric tool, capable of catching small fish. A master of fusion, he combines ancient stone-craft with high-velocity mechanics, utilizing a legendary magic ladder to scale the shifting architecture of the realms. Whether brewing portal potions, harvesting floof-berries in Limbo with gemstone goggles, or aiding Buddy the Mouse, this walnut-loving student of the stars remains the hero who orchestrated the daring rescue of the world.

characters.name: Uncle Grisco
characters.description: A nomadic mole scientist and science teacher, this subterranean inventor resides in a home brimming with wondrous experiments, serving as a devoted confidant to the gopher folk. Within his laboratory of glowing elixirs and prismatic shadows, he fuses volcanic lore with the study of light to translate the planet's restless movements and the scarred history of cosmic craters. A wandering sage who maps both deep tectonic veins and celestial paths, he frequently emerges from his sprawling tunnels to share ancient botanical secrets and astronomical insights with his friend Seeker.

characters.name: Gravity Goldfish
characters.description: Once a soaring engine of atmospheric ruin that leveled metropolises, this sky-bound terror was transformed by a single Valentine into a silent, mended guardian. Now celebrated in song as a legendary "pretend fish," the reformed creature drifts peacefully through the clouds, keeping a gentle, protective watch over the world and its companion, Mini Mango.

characters.name: Astronauts
characters.description: Space travelers who journey from Earth to the Moon.

places.name: Earth
places.description: The ancestral cradle of humanity and the definitive anchor for the galactic mission, this rocky world pulses with a hidden molten core. As the home planet where the journey begins and ends, it serves as the foundational site for the Floofers’ true home and new school, bridging an ancient legacy with the education of a new generation.

places.name: Mars
places.description: Serving as the home of Julian and the primary destination for the Floofers’ quest for the mookball, this primordial red world hosts the Red Bolt landing site. Defined by the rhythmic thunder of giant volcanoes and the jagged edges of ancient impact craters, the ash-shrouded landscape rests under a hazy pink sky in a haunting silence—a world devoid of other human life where only the fire and dust endure.

places.name: Venus
places.description: A distant, parched world where ship sensors track a critical and relentless rise in temperature across the empty expanse; here, amidst the lifeless, searing dust, the crew follows a haunting trail of footprints.

places.name: Moon
places.description: An celestial body with low gravity where visitors bounce as they walk.

places.name: Outer Space
places.description: The vast, dark expanse beyond Earth’s atmosphere, a silent cradle for shooting stars where Mickey and Minnie journey to witness the celestial glow while the crew scours the void for lost rocks and wandering stars.
